learning_objectives: Define ethics and distinguish between major ethical frameworks
Identify the unique ethical challenges posed by digital technology
Apply utilitarian, deontological, and virtue ethics to technology dilemmas
readings: Ethics of Information Technology and Business Ch. 1 by Reynolds
The Trolley Problem in the Age of Self-Driving Cars (MIT Technology Review)

lecture_notes.introduction: Technology amplifies human action. What once affected a few people can now affect millions with a single line of code. Digital ethics asks: just because we can, does that mean we should?
key_concepts: Utilitarianism: greatest good for the greatest number
Deontological ethics: duty-based, some actions are inherently right or wrong
Virtue ethics: character and habits of the moral agent
The pace problem: technology evolves faster than ethical norms and laws
Stakeholder analysis in technology decisions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the ethical dimensions of a social media company's decision to change its algorithm to maximize engagement

review.value: Evaluate a tech decision by summing benefits and harms across all affected parties. The right action maximizes net benefit.
Laws and social norms evolve over decades; technology disrupts in months. This gap creates ethical gray zones where behavior is legal but potentially harmful.

learning_objectives: Explain the difference between privacy, confidentiality, and anonymity
Analyze how data collection practices affect individual privacy
Evaluate the trade-offs between convenience and privacy in digital services
readings: The Age of Surveillance Capitalism Ch. 1-3 by Shoshana Zuboff
GDPR: What It Means for You (summary article)

lecture_notes.introduction: Privacy is not about having something to hide. It is about maintaining autonomy over your own information. The digital economy runs on personal data, and understanding what is collected and how it is used is essential for informed digital citizenship.
key_concepts: Types of personal data: PII, behavioral data, metadata
Data collection methods: cookies, device fingerprinting, location tracking
Surveillance capitalism: how free services monetize user data
Privacy regulations: GDPR, CCPA, and their protections
The privacy paradox: people say they value privacy but act otherwise
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students audit their own Google or Apple account data download to see what has been collected about them
setup_requirements: Access to personal Google or Apple account (optional, alternatives provided)

review.value: Business model where human experience is harvested as raw material for prediction products sold to advertisers. The user is not the customer; they are the product.
Data minimization (collect only what's needed), purpose limitation (use only for stated purpose), right to erasure (right to be forgotten), consent must be informed and specific

learning_objectives: Explain how bias enters algorithms through data and design choices
Identify real-world cases of algorithmic discrimination
Evaluate proposed solutions for algorithmic fairness
readings: Weapons of Math Destruction Ch. 3-5 by Cathy O'Neil
Gender Shades: Intersectional Accuracy Disparities (Buolamwini & Gebru, summary)

lecture_notes.introduction: Algorithms are not neutral. They encode the values, biases, and blind spots of the people who build them and the data they are trained on. When algorithms make high-stakes decisions, biased outputs become systemic discrimination.
key_concepts: Sources of bias: historical data, proxy variables, sampling bias
Case study: facial recognition accuracy across demographics
Case study: hiring algorithms that discriminate against women
Feedback loops: biased predictions reinforcing biased outcomes
Fairness definitions: equality of outcome vs. equality of treatment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Testing a publicly available AI image classification system for demographic bias
setup_requirements: Web browser with access to AI demo tools

review.value: A variable that correlates with a protected category (race, gender) and introduces bias indirectly. Example: using zip code as a proxy for race in lending decisions.
Biased algorithm predicts higher crime in an area, police patrol more there, find more crime, data reinforces the original prediction. The bias is self-perpetuating.

class_schedule.topic: Artificial Intelligence: Capabilities, Limits, and Ethics
learning_objectives: Distinguish between narrow AI, general AI, and superintelligence
Identify current capabilities and limitations of large language models
Evaluate ethical concerns around AI-generated content, deepfakes, and automation
readings: AI Ethics by Mark Coeckelbergh, Ch. 3-5
On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots (Bender et al., summary)

lecture_notes.introduction: AI is transforming every industry, but public understanding ranges from utopian to dystopian. This session provides a grounded understanding of what AI can and cannot do, and examines the ethical questions that demand urgent attention.
key_concepts: Narrow AI vs. Artificial General Intelligence
How large language models work: pattern prediction, not understanding
AI hallucination: confident and wrong
Deepfakes and synthetic media: detection and implications
Automation and labor displacement: who benefits, who is harmed
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing outputs from an AI chatbot on factual questions to identify hallucinations and evaluate reliability
setup_requirements: Access to an AI chatbot (ChatGPT, Claude, or similar)

review.value: AI models generate plausible-sounding but fabricated information, including fake citations, because they predict likely text rather than retrieving verified facts.
Narrow AI excels at specific tasks (chess, translation, image recognition). AGI would match human-level reasoning across all domains. No AGI exists; current systems are narrow.

learning_objectives: Distinguish between copyright, patents, trademarks, and trade secrets
Explain fair use and its application to digital content
Evaluate the ethical debates around AI-generated content and copyright
readings: Ethics of Information Technology and Business Ch. 6 by Reynolds

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital technology makes copying trivially easy and distribution instant. This disrupts centuries of intellectual property law and raises new questions about ownership, creativity, and compensation in the digital age.
key_concepts: Copyright: protects original creative expression automatically
Fair use: four factors (purpose, nature, amount, market effect)
Creative Commons licenses: a spectrum of permissions
The debate: can AI create copyrightable works?
Digital piracy, DRM, and the ethics of access
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing whether specific uses of copyrighted material qualify as fair use using the four-factor test

review.value: 1) Purpose (educational/commercial), 2) Nature of the work (factual/creative), 3) Amount used (small portion/entire work), 4) Market effect (does it replace the original?)
Current legal consensus in the US: AI-generated works without human authorship cannot be copyrighted. But works with substantial human creative direction may qualify. The law is rapidly evolving.

learning_objectives: Propose governance frameworks for emerging technologies
Evaluate the roles of individuals, companies, and governments in digital ethics
Articulate a personal digital ethics code of conduct
readings: AI Ethics by Mark Coeckelbergh, Ch. 8-9
The EU AI Act: Summary and Implications (2024 analysis)

lecture_notes.introduction: Ethics is not just about identifying problems; it is about building solutions. This final session examines governance approaches and asks each student to define their own ethical commitments as a digital citizen and future professional.
key_concepts: Governance models: self-regulation, government regulation, co-regulation
The EU AI Act: risk-based regulation framework
Responsible AI principles: transparency, accountability, fairness, privacy
Individual responsibility: ethical consumption, data hygiene, speaking up
Ethics by design: building ethical considerations into technology from the start
lecture_notes.demonstration: Small group exercise designing an ethical review process for a hypothetical AI product launch

review.value: Unacceptable risk (banned: social scoring), High risk (regulated: hiring AI, medical devices), Limited risk (transparency required: chatbots), Minimal risk (unregulated: spam filters)
Incorporating ethical analysis at every stage of development, not as an afterthought. Includes diverse teams, impact assessments, and ongoing monitoring.

questions.question: A social media company collects location data from users to sell to advertisers, generating revenue that funds the free service. Apply utilitarian and deontological ethics to evaluate this practice. Which framework leads to a different conclusion and why?
questions.answer: Utilitarian analysis: The practice benefits many users (free service) and advertisers (targeted marketing) but harms users through privacy invasion and potential for misuse. If the aggregate benefit (billions using a free service) outweighs the aggregate harm (privacy loss, manipulation risk), a utilitarian might approve, though this depends on how you measure and weight the harms. Deontological analysis: Collecting and selling personal data without fully informed consent violates the duty to respect persons as ends in themselves, not merely as means to profit. A deontologist would likely condemn the practice regardless of the benefits because using people's data as a product to sell violates their autonomy. The frameworks can lead to different conclusions because utilitarianism evaluates consequences while deontology evaluates the nature of the action itself.

rubric.key: Explains how frameworks lead to different conclusions

questions.question: What is the 'privacy paradox' and what are two possible explanations for why it exists?
questions.answer: The privacy paradox is the observation that people consistently say they value privacy in surveys but behave in ways that contradict this, such as sharing personal information freely on social media or accepting terms of service without reading them. Two explanations: (1) Rational ignorance: the effort required to read and understand privacy policies for every service exceeds the perceived benefit, so people rationally skip them even though they care in principle. (2) Immediate gratification bias: the benefits of using a service are immediate and tangible, while the costs of privacy loss are abstract, uncertain, and delayed, leading people to discount future risks in favor of present convenience.

rubric.key: Correct definition of the privacy paradox
First explanation is plausible and well-reasoned
Second explanation is distinct and well-reasoned

questions.question: Explain how a feedback loop in predictive policing can perpetuate racial bias, even if the algorithm does not use race as an input variable.
questions.answer: A predictive policing algorithm trained on historical arrest data will identify areas with high past arrests as high-risk. If historical policing disproportionately targeted minority neighborhoods (more patrols, more stops, more arrests), the data reflects policing patterns rather than actual crime rates. The algorithm sends more officers to those neighborhoods, leading to more arrests there, which feeds back into the training data as evidence of high crime. Race is never an input variable, but zip code, neighborhood, and historical arrest rates serve as proxies that correlate with race. The loop is self-reinforcing: biased data produces biased predictions that produce more biased data.

rubric.key: Explains how historical bias enters training data
Identifies proxy variables that correlate with race

questions.question: What is an AI hallucination? Why does it happen, and why is it a significant ethical concern when AI is used for research, journalism, or medical advice?
questions.answer: An AI hallucination occurs when a language model generates information that is factually false but presented with the same confidence as accurate information, such as citing non-existent research papers or fabricating statistics. It happens because language models work by predicting the most likely next word based on patterns in training data, not by retrieving verified facts. The model has no mechanism to distinguish between true and false statements. This is ethically concerning because: in research, fabricated citations could undermine scientific integrity; in journalism, false claims presented as fact could spread misinformation to large audiences; in medicine, incorrect information could lead to harmful health decisions. The core ethical issue is that AI hallucinations are difficult for non-experts to detect, creating a false sense of authority and reliability.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of why it occurs
Ethical concerns in at least two domains

major_assignments.title: Digital Ethics Case Study Analysis
major_assignments.description: Select a real-world digital ethics controversy from the provided list or propose your own (with approval). Conduct an in-depth ethical analysis applying multiple ethical frameworks, identifying all stakeholders, evaluating the trade-offs, and proposing a well-reasoned ethical recommendation.
objectives: Apply at least two ethical frameworks to a real technology controversy
Identify and analyze the perspectives of all affected stakeholders
Evaluate trade-offs between competing values such as privacy vs. security or innovation vs. fairness
Propose a specific, actionable ethical recommendation with justification
deliverables: Stakeholder map identifying all affected parties and their interests
Ethical framework analysis applying at least two frameworks to the case
Recommendation with specific policy or design proposals
References (at least 6 credible sources including academic and journalistic)

criteria: Thorough, accurate description of the controversy
At least two ethical frameworks applied correctly
Analysis shows genuine engagement, not superficial application
Each stakeholder's interests and concerns articulated
Stakeholder map is clear and informative
Rigorous ethical analysis demonstrating deep engagement with the material
Multiple frameworks applied with sophistication, not just name-dropping
All stakeholders considered fairly with genuine attempt to understand competing perspectives
Actionable recommendation that is both ethically grounded and practically feasible

participation_and_discussion.description: Active, informed participation in class discussions and debates

quizzes.description: Assessment of ethical concepts and ability to apply frameworks

weekly_reflections.description: Short weekly reflections on digital ethics topics in the news

case_study_analysis.description: In-depth ethical analysis of a real-world digital ethics controversy

personal_ethics_code.description: Final project articulating a personal digital ethics code with justification

course_policies.attendance: This is a discussion-intensive course. More than 3 unexcused absences will lower your participation grade to zero. Active participation means contributing thoughtfully, not just attending.
course_policies.late_submissions: Reflections are due weekly and cannot be submitted late, as they relate to current events. The case study has staged deadlines with 10% per day late penalty.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussions are collaborative. Written work must be individual. You may discuss ideas with classmates but must write independently.
course_policies.academic_integrity: In a course about ethics, academic integrity is paramount. Plagiarism or fabricated sources will result in course failure. AI tools may be used for research but not for writing your analysis. Disclose all AI assistance.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Internet access for research and discussion forums. No specialized software required.
